Name: Suddenly, Last Summer
Review:
Will Kempner
Hollywood Auteurs
N17094937

Suddenly, Last Summer

	Suddenly, Last Summer directed by Joseph Mankiewicz is an adaptation of Tennessee William’s one-act play of the same name. Through a number of interesting choices, Mankiewicz truly channeled his inner auteur and made a film as odd and original as any I’ve seen. 
	The first thing that stands out to me is the way he structured the film. One of the first things I was taught in screenwriting classes was to keep scenes brisk and keep the tempo of the film moving. If a conversation had to be long, why not let set it through multiple rooms? This film totally subverts this rule by having the entire film take place in only a couple of long scenes.
	This decision clearly seems a result of adapting a play, and it adds an epic sense of importance to each scene. The first long scene, which takes place in Violet Venable’s “garden of creation” gives Violet’s story an almost timeless feel. Much of the film involves characters discussing things that happened off-screen before the film began, describing a character we never meet. 
	Coupling this decision with the films subject matter, mental stability, adds greater uncertainty to the audience. Who is telling the truth? What really happened to Sebastian? Is Catherine really crazy or is her planned lobotomy just part of Violet’s plan? This kind of story telling may have influenced later films like Shutter Island, that deal with asylum inmates uncertain motivations and mental states.
	One very interesting decision is late in the film when Catherine reveals what happened to Sebastian. Instead of just having characters reminisce and talk about other characters we actually get to see what happened. This elimination of the ambiguity actually made things even more confusing for me. One moment we are in a film that feels at least somewhat grounded in reality, and next thing you know we are watching a flashback that depicts a character being eaten by savage cannibals on a tropical island. 
	Using a flashback like that is another great subversion used by the director. Where the rest of the film acted like a play, few locations and mostly just dialogue. This scene suddenly takes us out of the present and uses a faded split screen to depict the horrifying memory Catherine has. This is a great example of storytelling only possible through film, transcending the limitations of theater. 
	This meditation on Freudian Themes results in an odd story where everyone seems to be both mentally unstable and overtly sexual with each other. At any moment I thought these characters could either break out into hysterics or an orgy. This mismatch of love and hate, sanity and insanity, scientific medicine and tribal sacrifice creates a a powerful juxtaposition for the characters to navigate through.
	It’s true that the films subject matter can seem odd and offensive to a modern audience, we witness a homosexual man get seemingly justifiably eaten because of his longings. But this all contributes to it being a one of a kind film that could only have been made in its time by the minds who made it.

Name: Seconds
Review:
Coming to HBO MAX in 2020, Olivia Wilde's "Seconds."

Based on the infamous 1966 film, this mini series is both a sequel and a reboot. The Reborn Organization has been quietly working for nearly 60 years, helping high paying customers start over and 'reboot' their own lives.

SUE RITEMAN, a suburban housewife / empty nester with an absent- husband, gets a text that changes her life forever. She decides to get reborn with a beautiful new identity (Olivia Wilde, who will be lead both in front of and behind the camera.) But things quickly go awry when she realizes that her issues were more than skin deep.

The pilot ends where the film ends, with the Organization deciding to use her as a cadaver for future clients but at the last second she escapes. Eventually she finds a rebellious group of former Reborns in exile (consisting of Elvis, Tupac and even Epstein.)

Over the course of the riveting, edge of your seat season, "Sue" must outrun one of the most powerful groups on Earth while trying to right the wrongs of her past.

Name: Space Jam: A New Legacy
Review:
Following the surprising results of the 1960 Presidential Election, JFK was sworn in as the next president of the United States. An outsider from the older Yale-adjacent Washington power brokers, Kennedy seemed to signal a youthful and fresh perspective to the nation's increasingly stale Cold War policies.

Alongside his "New Frontier" appointees, JFK was determined to steer a new legacy, breaking with the conventional wisdom of the time. But he would soon learn an important lesson, having the top job did not always guarantee the powers that should come along with it.

Entrenched ideologies and individuals proved hard to outmaneuver, and Kennedy frequently found his orders to be undermined from within. Corporate interests often superseded his agenda, and close allies would soon reveal themselves to have ulterior motives.

While we may never know exactly what happened, it is clear that backroom meetings and cash exchanges took place prior to his shocking and early termination. What we do know is that the establishment and their powerful clients got the results they wanted, while the people lost a great opportunity.

"Those who make peaceful revolution impossible will make violent revolution inevitable." - John Fitzgerald Kennedy

Name: A Face in the Crowd
Review:
In Elia Kazan's A Face In The Crowd, the arc of Griffith's bum-becomes-stud Larry "Lonesome" Rhodes is that of a warning, one as loud and insincere as any of his infamous laughs. By the time Marcia Jeffries confronts a drunken and unconsolable Rhodes in his empty ballroom, the audience is asked to be devastated and resigned alongside her because the man whom she had saved from the mid-20th century carceral state of the American South had blown the once in the lifetime opportunity that luck, and herself, had given him. Rhodes had succumbed to fame, capitalism, and women. He was a broken soul before we met him, all that fame and power was bound to corrupt. Marcia had no choice but to leave on the hot mic and expose his hypocrisy to the world. 

In the kino's last reel, Rhodes finally lives up to his moniker and screams the loneliest of howls into the night, Marcia takes a car and heads off. Why does she not go back? Is he as lost of a cause as the film would lead us to assume? Or is it that Marcia did not want to confront the realities that such a conversation would uncover? 

In the sobriety of the morning light, would Rhodes not have begun to question why a Sarah Lawrence graduate who used her families inherited wealth to fund him as a pet project, would decide to end his career? She had found him in prison for disorderly conduct, still drunk, and had him perform a song and dance for her audience of middle class radio listeners. But now the fun was over, it was time for him to head back down. 

Marcia had no problem enabling his obvious addiction when it benefited her bottom line (and most importantly gave her gilded life an edge.) Despite Mr. Rhodes late-career diatribes calling his audience sheep, we know from earlier scenes that he has empathy for the poor and an acute understanding of corporate power and their stranglehold on the lower classes. Knowing this perhaps Mel (the over-educated late night tv writer-turned-novelist) should not have condescendingly lectured Rhodes on his coming slump, but instead offered to help someone who was not used navigating elite spaces and was obviously suffering from a lifetime of addiction brought on by neglect from the same systems that pay for him to write late night jokes. 

A few years before the release of A Face In The Crowd, Mr. Kazan would testify at the House Un-American Actives Committee identifying communists among his peers. Orson Welles infamously said "He is a man who sold to McCarthy all his companions at a time when he could continue to work in New York at high salary..." The same could be said for Marcia and Mel. Watering the seed that let Rhode's career bloom, they were forced to confront the reflection of the society that had benefited them both lavishly. Unable to face the mirror, they ran away to become one with their crowd.

Name: Baby Invasion
Review:
“BABY INVADERS” Review (2025) – Harmony Korine’s Brain Rot Masterpiece | 16yo Streamer Vibes

Yo. So I just got back from watching Baby Invaders and… bro. What the ACTUAL hell did I just watch? Like no cap, I feel like I got MK-Ultra’d in the theater. Harmony Korine is either a genius or needs to be put on some kind of government list. Probably both.

Okay so first off—this isn’t a movie, it’s a vibe hack. Like, you think you’re just watching some weird experimental film with babies and aliens and dirt bikes, but then 20 minutes in your brain starts to melt. I felt my eyeballs doing a TikTok transition. I swear I heard the THX sound but like, backwards and dripping in slime.

Let me try to explain what Baby Invaders even is. Spoiler: I can’t. But I’ll try. It’s kind of about these freaky CGI babies who crash land in what looks like a GTA5 modded server—there’s no real plot, just vibes, guns, neon sweat, and people with voice modulators doing ASMR threats. One dude was dressed like Pikachu but was crying blood and whispering about 9/11 conspiracies. I think that was the main character? Maybe?

There’s a part where a baby gets launched out of a cannon into a gas station. Another scene has this guy in a racing helmet monologuing about Fortnite trauma while surrounded by slow-motion clips of pigs exploding. And I was just sitting there like, “Bro. What are we DOING right now??” But like… in a good way?

It kinda feels like when you’re 3 hours deep into a Discord call and someone starts screen sharing cursed YouTube videos and you’re too tired to log off. You know you should stop. But you can’t. It’s scary how easy it is to fall down the rabbit hole with this one. Like one minute you’re laughing at the baby with a cigarette, and the next you’re thinking “Wait… is this about God?”

No joke, there’s some real horror in here too. Not jumpscare stuff, but that deep-in-your-bones unease. Like Skinamarink but if it was edited by someone who mainlines Monster Energy and posts memes at 3am. There’s this scene where all the babies just stare at you—dead silent—for like, five full minutes. And I SWEAR I saw one blink in real time. My friend left the theater. Just got up and dipped. And honestly? Respect.

Also—THE SOUND DESIGN? Holy. I felt like I was being hacked. Korine uses like, pirate radio frequencies and broken MIDI files and weird animal growls layered under dialogue. Some of the voices are just auto-tuned gibberish. One baby speaks in what sounded like Minecraft eating sounds but reversed. It shouldn’t work. But it does.

If you told me this was secretly a message from the dark web I’d believe you. There’s even this ARG vibe—like I left the theater and now I keep seeing weird symbols in real life. Is that part of the movie? Am I still in it? Bro help me.

Okay so is Baby Invaders “good”? I don’t even know what that means anymore. It’s not a movie you recommend. It’s a movie you dare people to watch. But I will say this: I’ve never seen anything like it. And I’m not gonna forget it. And now I’m scared to sleep.

Final Score: 10/10 if your brain is already cooked. 2/10 if you still use Facebook.

Watch it with headphones. Or don’t. Your choice. But if the babies start whispering to you in real life… don’t answer.

Name: The Fantastic 4: First Steps
Review: The Walt Disney Company’s Fantastic Four: The First Steps tells the story of a close-knit group who, after a space expedition, become powerful wealthy elites. During the first of many montages, we learn that their superior material capabilities have allowed them to convince all of the world’s nations to disarm themselves. This is depicted as an aspirational development. Only Doctor Doom’s homeland of Latveria abstains, framing the straightforward choice of a small nation to maintain military force against hegemonic power as comically villainous. The mainstream media of this retro-future world is obsessed with the foursome, and prominently features these unelected strongmen. They occupy all levels of culture and are seen as both political thought leaders and pop idols. This cult of personality clearly begins at a young age — early indoctrination in the form of an animated television show. Soon an extraterrestrial arrives to warn of the imminent destruction of the planet. The “heroes” rush into space to confront the threat, only to be told they could save everyone if they sacrificed their unborn child. Confident in their place atop the hierarchy, the Four Pharaohs return to Earth and, with almost no hesitation, announce they have chosen the mass death of all history over even considering alternatives. Sue Storm, the invisible elite who dictates global policy, seems almost offended that her subjects are upset. The respectable option of at least going down with one’s ship seems off the table, as only this family of wealthy white, blonde people have the proprietary technology needed to get another solar system. In the end, the quartet of daemons offers to teleport the entire planet to another galaxy. To do this dangerous and unethical experiment, they demand the entire world reduce their electricity consumption. As expected the media uncritically pushes the mandate onto the population and the film itself glamorizes this austerity. They will return in Avengers: Doomsday.
